variables.V₀: EMF of the battery / initial voltage (V)
variables.V_C: Voltage across capacitor at time t (V)
variables.I: Current in the circuit at time t (A)
variables.q: Charge on capacitor at time t (C)

locked_facts: Charging: V_C(t) = V₀(1 - e^(-t/RC)), current I(t) = (V₀/R)e^(-t/RC)
Discharging: V_C(t) = V₀ e^(-t/RC), current I(t) = -(V₀/R)e^(-t/RC)
Time constant τ = RC: at t = τ, capacitor charges to 63.2% of V₀ (1 - 1/e)
At t = 5τ, capacitor is ~99.3% charged — considered fully charged
Charge on capacitor: q(t) = CV₀(1 - e^(-t/RC)) during charging
Energy stored in capacitor: U = ½CV² = q²/(2C)
During charging, half the energy from battery is dissipated in R as heat, half stored in C
truth_statements: The time constant τ = RC determines how fast the capacitor charges or discharges — larger τ means slower response
At t = 0 during charging, capacitor acts like a short circuit (V_C = 0, I = V₀/R maximum)
At t = ∞ during charging, capacitor acts like an open circuit (V_C = V₀, I = 0)
The charging current decreases exponentially because as V_C rises, the net voltage driving current (V₀ - V_C) decreases
Exactly half the energy supplied by the battery during charging is lost as heat in the resistor, regardless of R value

aha_moment.what_to_show: Two side-by-side plots: V_C(t) rising exponentially toward V₀ and I(t) decaying exponentially toward zero. At t = τ = RC, vertical line marks the 63% charge point. The V_C curve reaches ~63% of V₀ and the current drops to ~37% of initial. Five τ markers shown — by 5τ the capacitor is essentially fully charged.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: τ = RC marked prominently. 63% and 37% levels highlighted. Exponential nature visible — the rate of change itself decreases over time.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that charging is NOT linear — it slows down as V_C approaches V₀, because the driving voltage (V₀ - V_C) shrinks

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: RC circuit with battery V₀, resistor R, and uncharged capacitor C. Switch open. V_C = 0, I = 0. τ = RC calculated and displayed. V(t) and I(t) graph axes ready.
simulation_states.emphasis: Before switch closes: no charge, no current. τ = RC will govern how fast charging occurs.

simulation_states.label: Buildup
simulation_states.description: Switch closes. Current surges to I₀ = V₀/R. V_C starts rising from zero. Current starts decaying. V_C(t) and I(t) curves being traced. Charge flows onto capacitor plates — plate charge animation visible.
simulation_states.emphasis: Initially: all voltage across R, none across C. As C charges, voltage shifts from R to C.

simulation_states.label: Aha
simulation_states.description: At t = τ: V_C = 0.632 V₀ (63.2%), I = 0.368 I₀ (36.8%). At t = 5τ: V_C ≈ V₀, I ≈ 0. The exponential curves show the characteristic shape. Switch to discharge: V_C decays from V₀ to zero with same τ.
simulation_states.emphasis: 63% at one τ. Charging slows as V_C → V₀. Discharge is mirror image: V_C decays exponentially.

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive: adjust R and C to change τ. Toggle between charging and discharging. See how larger R or larger C slows the response. Energy bar shows energy stored in C vs energy dissipated in R.
simulation_states.emphasis: Double R or double C → τ doubles → charging takes twice as long. Energy: half to C, half to R (always).

common_misconceptions.belief: A capacitor charges linearly to V₀ in time τ
common_misconceptions.correction: Charging is exponential, not linear. At t = τ, the capacitor is only 63.2% charged (not 100%). It takes about 5τ to reach ~99%. The rate of charging decreases as V_C approaches V₀ because the driving voltage (V₀ - V_C) shrinks.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show the exponential V_C curve vs a linear line — they diverge significantly. Mark τ at 63%, not 100%.

common_misconceptions.belief: All the battery energy goes into the capacitor during charging
trigger_phrases: R mein energy waste nahi hoti
common_misconceptions.correction: Exactly HALF the battery energy is stored in the capacitor (½CV₀²), and the other half is dissipated as heat in the resistor. This 50-50 split is independent of the value of R — surprising but true.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show energy bars: battery supplies CV₀². Capacitor stores ½CV₀². Resistor dissipates ½CV₀². Always 50-50.

legend.meaning: Capacitor voltage during charging: V₀(1 - e^(-t/τ))
Energy split between C (stored) and R (dissipated)
